title: Filling gaps between floorboards with timber slivers
subtitle: Cut thin timber wedges and glue them into draught-causing gaps between old floorboards
excerpt: How to fill wide gaps between original floorboards using timber slivers rather than flexible filler. Slivers are the durable solution for gaps over 5 mm that filler cracks and drops out of.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Traditional UK joinery practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin tapered wedge of timber cut to match the width and depth of a gap between floorboards. Glued in place and planed or sanded flush when set.

content.text: Old floorboards shrink across their width as they dry out over decades. Gaps wider than 5 mm are too large for flexible filler to hold, filler at that width compresses with foot traffic, cracks, and drops through. A
timber sliver
fills the gap permanently and moves with the board over seasonal changes.
When to use it

Use slivers for gaps of 5 to 25 mm in original pine or hardwood floorboards. For gaps under 5 mm, flexible flooring filler or a mixture of sawdust and PVA works adequately. For gaps over 25 mm, or where boards are loose or cupped, the practical solution is to pull up the boards and re-lay them packed together before the slivers go in.
What you need

Timber for the slivers: a length of softwood offcut (PAR pine, 50 × 25 mm or similar) in a colour that roughly matches the floor, or a length of the same species if you can source it. Gaps in pine floors are best filled with pine. The sliver should be slightly thicker than the gap at the wide end and taper to a point over its 300 to 400 mm length.
Step 1, Measure the gap
Check the gap width at several points along its run. Long gaps are rarely parallel, the board tapers. Note the widest and narrowest measurements.
Step 2, Cut the slivers

Mark a taper on a length of offcut timber that runs from the widest gap measurement at one end down to a 1 to 2 mm edge at the other. Cut along the taper line with the tenon saw. Clean up the cut face with the utility knife or a smoothing plane if you have one. For long gaps, cut slivers in sections of 400 to 600 mm; full-length slivers over 1000 mm are difficult to drive in straight.
Step 3, Dry-fit, apply glue, and drive in

Test the sliver in the gap dry: it should enter easily at the thin end and tighten as the taper thickens. If it binds before reaching full depth, trim it slightly. Apply PVA wood glue to both faces of the sliver, not to the gap itself, since the gap faces are hard to reach. Drive the sliver down into the gap with light blows from the rubber mallet, working along its length. Leave the top surface standing slightly proud of the boards; the glue will hold it while it dries.
Step 4, Level when set

Wait 24 hours for the PVA to cure fully. Saw the proud end of the sliver flush with the saw held flat against the board surface (a Japanese pull-saw works well here; a tenon saw also works). Sand the sliver flush with the surrounding boards using the sanding block and 80-grit paper, working along the grain, then finish with 120-grit.
What it looks like when it's right

The sliver should be flush with the boards on either side, with no rocking or spring when pressed. A hairline line where the sliver meets each board is normal and will close or blend when the floor is sanded and finished.
Common mistakes
Sliver too wide: it splits when driven in. Cut thinner and re-try. A sliver that enters under light mallet taps is right.
Sliver pops out after a few days: the glue didn't bond because the gap faces were dusty or dirty. Remove, clean both faces with white spirit, let dry, and re-glue.
Using the wrong timber: a hardwood sliver in a pine floor stands proud after seasonal movement and catches the eye. Match the species as closely as possible.
